wheel alignment check on the vehicle can help identify issues with the rear suspension. Examining the rear toe angles, thrust angle, and camber measurements can help identify damage conditions. If toe and camber are within specification on one side, but not the other, the issue is likely isolated to one side. If the total toe is within spec, but individual toe angles are out of specification in opposite directions, it may indicate an axle positioning issue or diamond on a full frame vehicle. Close inspection and measurements of the suspension parts, their mounting locations, and the structure of the vehicle should be done. Castings can be made into nearly any shape or thickness, with variations in cross section and wall thickness. Castings are typically machined to a smooth surface at the attachment areas. Parts are generally attached to other parts with gas metal arc (GMA) fillet welds, bolts, rivet bonding, or a combination of methods.

Moisture Contamination – an open brake system will absorb moisture. The amount of moisture absorbed into the system depends on the humidity level and the length of time the system is open. A refractive, electronic tester, or paper test strips can be used to determine if the brake fluid is contaminated. A system that has been open for an extended period should be tested for moisture. Moisture greatly reduces the boiling point of brake fluid. Absorption of even 4 percent moisture can reduce the boiling point 50 percent. Heat can cause water in the brake fluid to change from a liquid to a gas. When this occurs, the brake pedal stroke is used up, compressing the gas. This condition could go unnoticed since the water vapor returns to a liquid as the system cools. Note: A contaminated system sho
Brake fluid may damage the finish and can be conductive if it absorbs enough moisture, and can cause fire if it gets into electrical connectors. Identify and cap any open connection points for the:

Hail Damage Identification – hail damage looks similar to door dings, but is generally found above the crown of the panel, unless there are high winds driving hail into all areas of a panel. Damage is generally only on one side of the vehicle, unless the vehicle is being driven during the storm. Hail generally does not chip paint. It will create a smooth dent with no breaks in the finish. If the hail halls on the panel edges, such as the front of the roof that meets the windshield, the paint film may crack. Depending on the thickness, refinishing panels may not handle the hail as well as the original finish. A high film thickness may result in possible chipping of the paint in a hail storm. content: Restraint System Servicing – damaged or deployed restraint systems almost always set a diagnostic trouble code (DTC). However, it is important to remember not all DTC’s will turn on a malfunction indicator lamp (MIL). Therefore, the first step when servicing vehicle restraint systems should be performing a system scan using a vehicle maker approved scan tool. This will allow you to retrieve stored DTCs, perform system diagnostics, and may help identify damaged system components that may not have visible signs of damage. It is also important to remember that any services performed on the restraint system will likely result in the need to calibrate the system and may require programming of the module, if replaced. term: Conductive Compressor Oil – conventional A/C systems that are belt
content: driven by the engine use polyalkylene glycol (PAG) compressor oil. PAG oil cannot be added to electric A/C systems because PAG oil attracts moisture, which conducts electricity. If sensors within the HV compressor detect a level of electricity being conducted, the entire HV system, and therefore the vehicle itself, will shut down. It the wrong oil is added to a part, that part may require replacement. If the A/C system is charged and operated with the wrong compressor oil, the entire A/C system, including the lines, may require replacement. Adding the wrong oil can happen as easily as the technician using a recovery/recycling/recharging machine for an HEV that was previously used for adding PAG oil to a conventional A/C system. Coolant Testers – the two types of coolant testers include refractive testers and specific gravity testers. A refractive tester works by placing a small amount of coolant on a prism sight glass and holding the tool up to the light. The strength of the coolant can be seen on a graduated scale. Compared to steel, aluminum will transfer heat across the panel about three times faster. Aluminum also has a much greater rate of thermal expansion compared to steel. Brake pad replacement considerations include the amount of material remaining on the pad. Each vehicle maker will have recommendations indicating when brake material should be replaced. Depending on whether the friction material is riveted or bonded affects when the brake material is replaced. When inspecting brake material thickness, a part with riveted brake material will require replacement when more material is remaining as compared to bonded brake material. This is because the attaching rivets will contact the rotor before the pad will wear away. Check the condition of the brake lining. Look for signs of contamination, cracks, or deterioration.

Switch (Circuit Control) – every circuit has a circuit control. A control can be a simple push switch that opens and closes a lamp circuit to turn on and off the lamp. Most switches on modern vehicles are an input to the computer that processes the user request and manages the operation of the load. Switches usually have two or more sets of contacts. Opening these contacts opens the circuit. Closing these contacts completes the circuit. Common problems with a switch after a collision can be identified using the collision advantage. These include cracked casings, loose internal contacts, and loose or damaged connections. In most of these cases, the switch should be replaced

term: Health Concerns – one of the most noticeable items when inspecting a flood
content: damaged vehicle is the odor of the interior. Mildew, mold, or decaying organic material, especially under the heated conditions that occur inside a vehicle, can create a pungent smell. Vehicles that have been exposed to floodwaters, particularly brackish water, saltwater, or river water, may have mold growth. Mold can start to grow beneath the carpeting, seats, floor mats, filters, and other areas that trap moisture. Floodwater may contain E.coli and other harmful bacteria. Vehicles that are not properly and promptly cleaned, dried, and disinfected will experience some sort of bacterial growth that can cause respiratory problems for vehicle drivers and/or repair technicians. Drying out the inside as quickly as possible is critical to limiting the amount of potential contamination

when repairing aluminum, heat will help soften the metal slightly to aid in straightening efforts and reduce the chance the panel will crack. When heating aluminum, consider the following points: aluminum does not change color when heated to its melting point. The approximate melting point of aluminum is 1200 degrees Fahrenheit (649 degrees Celsius). An aluminum part can be melted easily with some heat sources, such as an induction heater and open flame sources. Aluminum naturally forms an oxide coating when exposed to air; this oxide melts at about 3,700 degrees Fahrenheit (2,038 degrees Celsius). Heat spreads faster in aluminum than steel. Temperature is more critical than time. If the repair heating temperature is exceeded, the panel may become permanently softened, also referred to as

Purpose of Anchoring – anchoring holds undamaged points in correct alignment while resisting the pulling force and distributes pulling forces between multiple anchoring points. Generally, the more anchoring points closest to the location of the pull will receive more force compared to anchoring there are, the less force is applied to each. However, the anchoring points closest to the location of the pull will receive more force compared to anchoring locations farther from the pull. For this reason, additional anchoring may be required closer to the source of the pull. Anchoring also allows pulling forces to be focused on the area of damage. Note: Proper anchoring is essential for correcting structural misalignment

Brake Assist Systems – vacuum and hydraulic are two common automotive brake assist systems. Vacuum assist brakes have a vacuum assist that contains a diaphragm. Vacuum assist brakes apply vacuum to one side of the diaphragm and atmospheric pressure to the other. Vacuum is obtained from the engine intake manifold. They may require an additional vacuum pump to supply adequate vacuum on some engines. Some vehicles use hydraulic power, instead of vacuum, to assist in the application of the brakes. MacPherson Strut – a strut style suspension utilizes a strut with a coil spring to support the weight of the vehicle and maintain wheel position. A strut assembly consists of a strut hosing with a strut rod, upper mount/bearing, and spring. Additional parts include the steering knuckle, hub and bearing assembly, lower control arm with ball joint, and stabilizer bar and links (if equipped). The strut mount attaches to the top of the strut rod and holds the coil spring in position. The strut mount also secures the strut assembly to the vehicle. Note: the strut also acts as a ride damper or shock absorber.

ways of measuring ride height include measuring from the ground up to specified points on the chassis. It should be done with a level surface with the wheels pointed straight ahead. mounting points helps identify whether the suspension mounts are in the correct locations and identifies damaged suspension system parts. This ensures that a vehicle may be aligned. If mounts or parts are damaged, the correct angles for steering and suspension, such as caster, camber, toe, steering axis inclination (SAI), or thrust angle, may not be able to be achieved. Note: Measuring suspension points can reduce cycle time by reducing the number of times a vehicle is sent out for alignment. Then, consider calibrating without the cover. Did it calibrate? What was the difference? You are not done yet. This just eliminated a possible cause. Was the cover repaired or refinished? The final calibration must be done with a properly refinished bumper in place. Finding the cause is a repetitive process in which you ask questions and then confirm or deny the information. Continue through this cycle until you find the root cause.

content: Double Wishbone – a double wishbone front suspension is a modified SLA design. This suspension features upper and lower control arms (wishbones) to position the wheel, the steering knuckle is taller than on a typical SLA suspension. This suspension uses a fixed position strut with coil spring that typically has a damper fork on the bottom to support the vehicle’s weight. The damper fork connects the strut to the lower control arm and allows the axle shaft to pass through its center. Double wishbone suspensions may have a radius arm attached to the lower control arm for positioning the lower ball joint at the correct position. Note: the strut assembly does not control wheel position on the double wishbone suspension

This type of repair is possible because the visible damage is mostly air trapped between the outer layer of glass and the plastic laminate. deterrent system operates when the doors are locked, either manually or remotely. The system monitors movement, noise, latches, and/or vehicle movement, and sets off an alarm when specific criteria are met. On some systems, the switches are checked three times per second to make sure switches are closed. These systems may also have a movement sensor that detects movement inside the vehicle. This particular system requires windows to be closed for proper operation. Some systems have a heartbeat sensor. The heartbeat sensor is used to detect the presence of someone inside the vehicle, with an indicator on the key fob.

term: Operation by Motion Sensor – the hands
content: free access system only operates when the key fob is within range and only under specific conditions. Motion sensors are programmed to respond to a specific sequence of movement. In general, this is the user approaching the vehicle, and the foot kicking in and then out. This is to prevent the door from opening while the vehicle is in motion, or when animals, balls, or other objects pass near the sensor. As an additional safety feature, the key fob must be outside the vehicle. Once triggered, the sensors alert the control module, which sends a command to the electric motor to either open or close the door.

Electrical Gloves – electrical gloves consist of two parts, the leather outer shell and the rubber inner shell. The leather outer shell provides no protection from electricity, but it does protect the rubber inner glove from being damaged by sharp objects. Gloves should be stored in the box or bag provided by the manufacturer. Direct sunlight will dry out the rubber and cause it to crack. The gloves should never be stored when folded, compressed, or inside out, as this will cause the gloves to crease and crack. Never use either part of the electrical insulation gloves for anything except when working with high voltage. Contaminating the gloves with engine oil or chemicals will degrade the rubber and reduce the insulating properties

content: Database Enhancement Gateway (DEG) – the Database Enhancement Gateway (DEG) is an initiative to help improve quality and accuracy of collision repair estimates. The DEG uses proactive feedback from the collision repair industry, and other “end users,” to the information provides that supply the databases for estimating products. The DEG offers users a more standardized and streamlined process for the generation of database inquiries. The DEG Administration serves an advocacy role, processing all submitted databases inquiries until they reach conclusion. The PCV valve is typically installed in the rocker arm cover or intake manifold, with a passage to the crankcase. It opens when the engine is running at idle. The PCV valve is pushed nearly closed when the engine is under load. When inspecting the PCV system, look for loose or damaged lines, connections, valves, and gaskets. content: Process Characteristics – the STRSW process is the main OEM welding method. Repair welds can be made to look just like original welds. Original construction can be duplicated in the field with less chance of error compared to other welding methods. With STRSW, there is minimal sparking. Though there is some sparking with some welds, there is much less sparking than with other welding processes. Vehicle and personal protection are still required. STRSW requires less preparation in some instances. For example, there is no need for drilling holes in replacement panels, such as when making gas metal arc (GMA) plug welds. No filler metal is required. It is unnecessary to dress the weld in most applications

Electric Motor – using energy from the HV battery, the electric motor drives the vehicle wheels. Electric motors perform both the drive and regeneration functions. In an electric car, there is no alternator. The motor in an electric car acts as both the motor and an alternator. That’s one of the reasons why electric cars are so unique. When your foot is on the accelerator, energy from the battery is used to power the motor, which supplies torque to the gears, which rotate the tires. When your foot comes off the accelerator, the motor turns into an alternator, and this energy may be used to help recharge the battery. Electric motors are capable of very high torque output at startup and across a wide rpm range

content: Importance and Benefits of Blueprinting – a blueprint for collision repair is like a blueprint for building a house. The construction blueprint and collision repair blueprint identify all the parts, equipment, labor, workforce, and workflow required to get the job done before any work is authorized to begin. Both blueprints contain thorough details for the Statement of Work, leaving nothing open to interpretation. The blueprinting process improves estimate accuracy, reduces repair delays, improves productivity, and reduces cycle time, which leads to improved customer satisfaction index (CSI) scores.

Automatic Windshield Wipers – an automatic windshield wiper system uses a rain sensor. The rain sensor may also be called a moisture sensor. These are commonly attached directly to the windshield. The sensor detects the amount of moisture on the windshield by sending an infrared ray through the windshield. The wet windshield deflects the infrared ray, sending a signal to the wiper motor module to start the wipers. The wiper switch may have to be turned on or adjusted to the automatic setting to work

term: Setback and Caster – this section goes over what setback and caster are
content: What is a Setback? – setback is when a wheel is moved back in the chassis compared to the same wheel on the opposite side of the vehicle. This causes the wheelbase on the side with setback to be shorter than the wheelbase on the opposite side. Setback of a front wheel will typically cause a change in caster for that wheel. The only exception to this would be if both the upper and lower pivot points are pushed back an equal amount. This would cause wheel setback, but no caster change. This is more likely on solid axle front suspension.

Fans – airflow moving through the condenser is required to remove heat from refrigerant and to allow for A/C system peak performance. The engine and radiator cooling fans are typically the primary airflow source when stationary or moving at low speeds. Additional airflow may be created with fans located in front of the condenser pushing air through it. Electric fans are most common and typically controlled by the PCM as needed. When fans are positioned in front of the condenser, the fans push air through the condenser. Pushing fans may be used when sufficient airflow cannot be obtained with air deflectors or airflow through the grille. When fans are positioned behind the condenser, the fans pull the air through. term: structural parts, such as exterior body panels
content: Heat Exposure – more intense fires will weaken the steel, while lower heat will leave the steel properties as originally designed. Knowing which is the case, of course, would require determining how much heat the part was exposed to. If the fire was quickly extinguished and the paint on the part is mostly intact, it is likely there was not enough heat to affect the structural strength of the steel. Direct Current (DC) – with DC circuits, current flows in only one direction. Typically, DC power comes from batteries that have negative and positive terminals. Current is the flow of electrons from the negative side of a battery through the circuit to the positive side of the battery. On most low voltage systems, less than 60 volts DC, the negative battery terminal is connected to the vehicle chassis. This is called grounding. High voltage systems, 60 volts or higher, are isolated from the vehicle body and chassis on both the positive and negative side of the system

Current – current is the rate of flow of electricity, the quantity of electrons moving past a fixed point in one second. It is measured in amperes or amps. The symbol for current is A or I in equations. There are two types, direct current (DC) and alternating current (AC)

Ohm’s Law – in most situations, you will not need to use Ohm’s Law to perform your job. The Ohm’s Law Formula does not provide a great visual of the relationship between voltage, current, and resistance. An understanding of this relationship is very important when working around high voltage (HV) systems. With Ohm’s Law, if we know two of the circuit values, we can solve for the third value. For example: if we know what the voltage and resistance are in the circuit, we can solve for the current in the circuit. Voltage divided by resistance equals current. content: lock brake system (ABS) and engine control modules, ACC systems use an ACC module to process radar information and determine if a forward vehicle is present. Work Hardening Rate – steel parts used in unitized structures gain a good deal of their final strength through work hardening when parts are formed. Collision damage further work hardens the part and the straightening process adds to it again. This limits the amount of straightening that should be done, because even if the part is restored to its correct shape, the mechanical properties and strength of the part may be significantly different than intended. Work hardening also tends to make a part more brittle and prone to cracking when straightened

is a nonfusion process which generates heat from 1,675 degrees F to 1,866 degrees F. This allows the electrode to flow and bond to the surface of the metal. content: Steering Gear Box – the steering gear box receives input from the steering column shaft. The rotation of the steering wheel is converted to the lateral movement of the steering linkage. The worm gear, which receives direction from the shaft, has spiral grooves that move a ball nut back and forth. The teeth on the ball nut mesh with the sector shaft gear teeth and move the sector shaft when the worm gear is rotated. Most late model vehicles use gear boxes with recirculating balls. The recirculating balls, which basically function like ball bearings, assist the movement between the ball nut rack and the sector gear.

content: Kink vs. Bend – the kink vs bend rule is no longer as simple as it sounds. A part may not look like it is kinked, but it may not be able to be straightened without cracking. If the part is bent, but the thickness or strength of the metal will not allow for straightening without leaving an area of permanent deformation, then replacement, either partial or complete, would be appropriate. A part collapsed in these areas usually requires replacement since a straightened collapse zone may not reach the same way in another collision

Roof Crush Resistance – roof crush resistance requirements are from Federal Motor Vehicle Safety Standard (FMVSS) 216. This standard establishes strength requirements for the passenger compartment roof to reduce deaths and injuries due to the crushing of the roof into the occupant compartment in rollovers. Previous requirements called for a roof crush resistance of 1.5 times the vehicle weight. Current requirements call for a roof crush resistance of 3.0 times the vehicle weight. Another testing organization, the Insurance Institute for Highway Safety (IIHS), requires a roof crush resistance of 4.0 times the vehicle weight to achieve a “Good” rating. The IIHS rating is only a consumer advisory, not a regulation

working with aluminum is different from working with mild steel. For instance, aluminum has less memory than a mild steel part. Once moved, aluminum cannot be easily moved back to its previous shape. Keep in mind that thicker material is more susceptible to crack when straightened. Heat may be an option for straightening aluminum as long as the parts are not heated above 450 degrees Fahrenheit. It is also important to know that the workability of aluminum varies with alloys. Some alloys are easier to repair than others. Heating and Straightening Considerations

content: Vehicle Repair Tolerance – repair tolerance is used during straightening process to determine the specific location of a measuring point. The repair tolerance varies between vehicle makers. For example, some North American vehicles may have a little more tolerance compared to some European or Asian vehicles. Previously, 3 or 5 mm tolerances were typically acceptable on older model vehicles. However, this recommendation is not as common with late model vehicle design. Today, vehicle structures overall have a tighter tolerance regardless of the vehicle maker
